feature_name: C64x+ VLIW core and instruction set
description: The device integrates a TMS320C64x+ fixed-point DSP core with an eight-issue VLIW pipeline. It supports software pipelining, saturation arithmetic, bit manipulation, and SIMD-style operations on 16-bit lanes. Harvard architecture with separate program and data paths sustains high throughput. L1 caches and a configurable L2 minimize external memory stalls. Deterministic real-time behavior requires careful scheduling and cache planning.
evidence: CPU/ISA reference; Device overview
dependencies: None; optional cache and memory configuration during init
Remarks: Follow TI calling conventions; consider pipeline hazards; warm instruction/data caches before latency-critical ISRs.

description: An on-chip L1P cache stores recently executed instructions to reduce fetch latency. Typical capacity is 32 KB with line-based allocation. It is transparent to software but benefits from code layout that improves locality and prefetch behavior. L2 can augment instruction storage as SRAM or cache. Locking critical code in L2 improves determinism for hard real-time.
evidence: Caches and on-chip memory; L1P description
dependencies: Optional cache configuration via startup code
Remarks: Keep tight loops contiguous; consider locking timing-critical code in L2 SRAM.

description: The L1D cache accelerates access to frequently used data. It typically provides 32 KB capacity tightly coupled to load/store units. Software should structure data to encourage spatial and temporal locality. DMA traffic must be coordinated to avoid cache-coherency hazards. L2 configuration complements L1D for predictable latency.
evidence: Caches and on-chip memory; L1D description
dependencies: Cache maintenance or non-cacheable regions when using DMA
Remarks: Align and pad data to cache lines; writeback/invalidate around DMA buffers as needed.

description: The L2 memory can be configured as SRAM, cache, or a mix to balance capacity and determinism. Using L2 as SRAM allows locking critical code and data outside replacement effects. As cache, it extends the working set at lower latency than external memory. Partitioning must be chosen early and held stable across builds. Proper L2 use is foundational to predictable real-time behavior.
evidence: L2 memory subsystem; Device memory map
dependencies: Startup configuration of L2 and memory attributes
Remarks: Reserve L2 SRAM for ISRs, stacks, and DMA descriptors for determinism.

description: IDMA performs fast internal transfers between L2 and L1 without EDMA overhead. It operates under CPU control for small, latency-sensitive moves. It helps stage code/data into L1 caches or SRAM deterministically. Programming is simpler than EDMA but limited to on-chip spaces. It complements EDMA for fine-grained copy operations.
evidence: CPU subsystem: IDMA registers
dependencies: None; coordinate with cache settings
Remarks: Use IDMA for short deterministic moves; prefer EDMA for large or peripheral-driven transfers.

description: The controller provides access to external DDR2 SDRAM compliant with JEDEC standards. Initialization requires programming timing registers and issuing the JEDEC power-up and mode register sequence. Refresh interval and any PHY/VTP calibration must match the board memory. Post-init pattern tests validate timing margins. After bring-up, caches and DMA can safely target DDR2.
evidence: DDR2 controller: SDCR, SDTIMx, SDRCR; Initialization sequence
dependencies: PSC enable for DDR2; stable PLL/clock; board-specific DDR2 parameters
Remarks: Program timings from the memory datasheet and verify with pattern tests before enabling high-throughput traffic.

description: EDMA3 provides high-throughput, non-blocking data movement between memory and peripherals. Transfers are described by PaRAM sets supporting linked and chained scatter-gather operations. Channels map to queues and transfer controllers that arbitrate bandwidth. Completion and errors are reported via IPR/ICR and CCERR. QDMA enables CPU-triggered copies with minimal overhead.
evidence: EDMA3 CC/TC: DCHMAP, DMAQNUM, EER/EESR, IPR/ICR, CCERR
dependencies: PSC enable for EDMA CC/TC; INTC mapping; optional peripheral event routing
Remarks: Use linked PaRAM for scatter-gather; clear completion in ICR after reading IPR; keep descriptors coherent and aligned.

description: The Ethernet subsystem implements a Layer-2 switch with two external ports and one host port. An Address Lookup Engine (ALE) provides MAC/VLAN tables and basic policing. Extensive statistics counters support diagnostics. An MDIO controller manages external PHY link discovery and configuration. Packet I/O uses DMA-based descriptor rings.
evidence: Ethernet switch/MDIO: MDIO_CONTROL, MDIO_USERACCESSx; ALE/VLAN sections
dependencies: PSC enable for 3PSW and packet DMA; PINMUX; MDIO clock; external PHYs; EDMA/packet DMA setup
Remarks: Initialize MDIO and confirm PHY links before traffic; configure ALE for host MACs/VLANs; ensure descriptor rings are cache coherent.

description: The PCI block supports host-master and slave transactions to connect with a PCI host. Software configures the local configuration header, BARs, and command/status to expose internal windows. Interrupts and DMA cooperate for high throughput. Proper reset sequencing and host negotiation are required at bring-up. Address translation must match the host’s view.
evidence: PCI: configuration header, BARs, command/status; Reset/bring-up
dependencies: PSC enable; host reset sequencing; BAR/window setup; EDMA for bulk movement
Remarks: Validate BAR mappings and endianness; coordinate reset with the host; use EDMA for large transfers.

description: The UHPI allows an external host to directly access the DSP memory map. Control registers manage bus width, resets, and ready/hold handshaking. It supports interrupt-driven or polled transactions. Integration simplifies host boot and debug scenarios. Access arbitration must avoid contention with the core and DMA.
evidence: UHPI: HPIC, HPIA, HPID; Usage notes
dependencies: PSC enable; host timing/handshake; interrupt routing if used
Remarks: Program HPIC for correct width and reset state; validate handshake timing; isolate shared regions to avoid races.

description: VLYNQ provides a point-to-point serial link that maps remote resources into the local address space. It enables chip-to-chip connectivity with memory-mapped semantics. Configuration defines lane rates, framing, and address windows. Interrupts and error status support robust operation. Both ends must be configured consistently for link bring-up.
evidence: VLYNQ peripheral: link control/status; address window config
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; remote endpoint configuration; address window mapping
Remarks: Negotiate link parameters with the peer; verify window mappings; test with small transactions before enabling DMA.

description: McASP supports I2S, TDM, and DIT modes with independent clock and frame sync generators. Serializers can be assigned to transmit or receive. Data flows through RBUF/TBUF FIFOs with rich interrupt signaling. Clocks and pins are controlled via GBLCTL and SRCTL registers. EDMA is typically used to service RX/TX for low-overhead streaming.
evidence: McASP: GBLCTL, SRCTLn, RBUF/TBUF, interrupts
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; EDMA channel setup; INTC mapping
Remarks: Sequence the McASP state machine carefully; verify clock polarity and frame timing; size EDMA bursts to frame boundaries.

description: The SPI module provides master/slave transfers with programmable clock polarity and phase. Formats define word length and bit order for flexible peripheral interfacing. Data moves through SPIDATx and SPIBUF with optional interrupts. Chip-selects and delays are configurable. EDMA can offload high-rate streaming transfers.
evidence: SPI: SPIGCR0/1, SPIFMTx, SPIDELAY, SPIBUF, SPIDATx
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; chip-select wiring; optional EDMA/interrupts
Remarks: Reset and configure SPIGCR0/1 before enabling; choose CPOL/CPHA to match the slave; align buffers if using EDMA.

description: The I2C module supports standard and fast modes in master or slave operation. Clocking is set via prescaler and SCL low/high timing registers. Transfers are controlled by ICMDR with status in ICSTR for robust error handling. Interrupts cover ready, NACK, arbitration lost, bus busy, and completion. External pull-ups on SDA/SCL are mandatory.
evidence: I2C: ICOAR, ICSAR, ICPSC, ICCL/ICCH, ICCNT, ICMDR, ICSTR
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; external pull-ups; INTC mapping if interrupts used
Remarks: Handle NACK and arbitration lost per ICSTR; program ICCNT correctly; deassert STOP only after the last byte is acknowledged.

description: The UART is 16550-like with configurable word length, parity, and stop bits. FIFOs buffer RX/TX to reduce interrupt rate. Baud is generated from the input clock using DLL/DLH divisors. Module enable and reset are controlled by PWREMU_MGMT. Interrupts or EDMA can service data depending on throughput targets.
evidence: UART: THR/RBR, LCR, FCR, DLL/DLH, IER/IIR, PWREMU_MGMT
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; clock reference for baud; INTC mapping
Remarks: Reset FIFOs before enabling; validate baud error; enable EDMA only when sustained rates justify the setup.

description: The GPIO module exposes up to 32 pins configurable as inputs or outputs. Direction is set per bit and data is written via SET_DATA/CLR_DATA or read via IN_DATA. Edge detection supports rising and falling edges with latched status for interrupts. Bank-level enables and status registers control interrupt delivery. GPIO can source EDMA sync events in certain configurations.
evidence: GPIO: DIR, SET/CLR_DATA, IN_DATA, SET/CLR_RIS_TRIG, BINTEN, INTSTAT01
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; INTC mapping; optional EDMA trigger usage
Remarks: Enable via PSC before access; clear edge flags after servicing; debounce externally if needed.

description: The general-purpose timer provides a 64-bit counter that can be split into two 32-bit timers. Period and control registers configure one-shot or periodic operation. Interrupts can be generated on compare or overflow events. The timer supports timestamps, periodic ticks, PWM-like waveforms, and capture. Proper prescaler and mode selection yield accurate timing.
evidence: Timer: TIM12/TIM34, PRD12/PRD34, TCR, TGCR, INTCTL
dependencies: PSC enable; INTC mapping; optional PINMUX for capture/PWM
Remarks: Select 64-bit or dual 32-bit mode early; compute prescalers from input clock; keep ISR short.

description: The main PLL multiplies the reference oscillator to generate CPU and system clocks. Dividers create peripheral clocks that feed modules such as GPIO and timers. Reprogramming requires a defined sequence with command and status handshaking. Clock stability must be ensured before enabling peripherals or external memory. Downstream timing depends on the chosen PLL rates.
evidence: PLLC: PLLCTL, PLLM, PLLDIVx, PLLCMD, PLLSTAT
dependencies: Stable reference clock; correct programming order; integration with PSC sequencing
Remarks: Follow the documented lock/unlock sequence; poll PLLSTAT; update peripheral dividers coherently.

description: The PSC manages power and clock gating of modules grouped into domains. Each peripheral has an LPSC state machine that must reach ENABLE before register access. Transitions are requested via PTCMD and observed through PTSTAT and MDSTAT. Correct sequencing avoids bus stalls and reduces power at idle. Software uses the PSC during initialization and mode changes.
evidence: PSC: PTCMD, PTSTAT, MDCTL[n], MDSTAT[n]
dependencies: Required by all peripheral drivers; domain sequencing per SoC integration
Remarks: Always enable a module via PSC before touching its registers; poll for transition completion; gate unused modules.

description: Many pins are multiplexed between peripheral functions and GPIO. Access to certain system registers requires unlocking KICK registers with device-specific keys. Correct mux selection ensures signal availability and prevents conflicts across modules. Boot configuration may set initial mux states that software should revisit. Reserved fields must be preserved when writing pinmux registers.
evidence: Device configuration/PINMUX; KICK0/KICK1 unlock keys
dependencies: KICK unlock sequence; ordered with PSC enables to avoid unsafe pin toggling
Remarks: Plan an early PINMUX configuration step; document conflicts; keep a board-specific mux table.

description: The device boots from sources such as I2C, SPI, UART, PCI, or HPI determined by strap pins. The ROM performs minimal clock and pin initialization to load a first-stage image. After transfer, control passes to application code in internal memory for full bring-up. Software typically reprograms PLL and enables required PSC modules. Capabilities can vary by ROM revision.
evidence: Reset and boot configuration; Boot pins
dependencies: Board strap pins; ROM revision; external boot media availability
Remarks: Keep the first-stage loader minimal; hand off to a unified board init that programs PLL/PSC/DDR deterministically.

description: The C64x+ interrupt model maps many system events into a small number of CPU interrupts. An event combiner aggregates peripheral events into fewer outputs to simplify routing. Device-level INTMUX selects which events drive INT4–INT15 lines. Proper ISR design clears peripheral status before acknowledging the controller. Efficient prolog/epilog and cache awareness reduce latency.
evidence: Interrupts and Event Combiner; INTMUX registers
dependencies: Peripheral interrupt enables; INTC mapping; optional event combiner usage
Remarks: Centralize vector table setup; keep ISR code small and defer to tasks; clear the correct peripheral flag first.

description: The core IDLE instruction gates the CPU clock until an enabled interrupt occurs. The PSC can further gate module or domain clocks to reduce consumption in standby. Wake sources must remain active and properly routed through the interrupt fabric. Software coordinates entry and exit to maintain data integrity and timing. Proper use reduces thermal load and improves energy efficiency.
evidence: IDLE instruction; PSC domain gating
dependencies: Interrupt routing for wake; PSC domain configuration
Remarks: Use IDLE in wait loops; validate that wake interrupts are unmasked; quiesce DMA before gating domains.

description: A JTAG interface provides real-time debug, trace, and halt capabilities. Some peripherals include emulation-aware controls to keep clocks active during halt. Debug sessions must consider side effects on timers, DMA, and external links. Boot timing and emulator attach order can influence behavior. Following guidance avoids interference with real-time operation.
evidence: Emulation notes; PWR/EMU-related options
dependencies: External emulator; toolchain integration
Remarks: Avoid halting during active transfers; enable emulation-aware options; script common debug attach sequences.

description: Peripherals expose status and error flags often cleared by writing one to the bit. The EDMA provides completion and error reporting via IPR/ICR and CCERR for robust flow control. Robust drivers check, log, and clear these conditions promptly in ISRs. Centralizing error paths simplifies diagnostics during bring-up. Documenting expected versus fatal errors improves field triage.
evidence: EDMA: IPR/ICR/CCERR; Peripheral status/clear regs
dependencies: Per-driver ISR and polling paths; logging infrastructure
Remarks: Unify error-reporting conventions; follow write-1-to-clear semantics; include counters and timestamps.

description: UNKNOWN. Public documentation for this device does not describe dedicated security or cryptographic accelerators. If any secure boot or access control exists, it is not detailed in standard manuals. Drivers should assume no hardware security engine is available by default. Reassess if later silicon revisions introduce such features.
evidence: UNKNOWN
dependencies: UNKNOWN
Remarks: UNKNOWN

description: Memory Attribute Registers control cacheability and allocation policy for external address regions. Proper setup ensures DMA buffers and MMIO windows have intended caching behavior. These settings complement L2 partitioning to balance performance and determinism. Misconfiguration can lead to stale data or bus faults under load. Adjustments are typically performed during early boot.
evidence: CPU memory attributes: MAR/MPAX overview
dependencies: Privileged CPU configuration; coordinated with cache and DMA usage
Remarks: Establish attribute tables during early init; mark MMIO as non-cacheable; verify with cacheability tests.}]}
